nodes.title: Explore the repo structure
nodes.description: Before changing anything, look around. Open the repo on GitHub.com or in your file explorer and understand what each folder is for. apps/ is for projects, docs/ is for business knowledge, workflows/ is for processes, journal/ is for notes.
nodes.tryIt: Open your repo and click through every folder. Read each README.md file inside them.

nodes.title: Run /setup to configure your OS
nodes.description: The /setup command is a 5-question interview that personalizes your repo. It writes your name, project, goals, and communication preferences to config.json so your AI tools know who you are and how to help. No AI tool? Edit config.json directly on GitHub.com.
nodes.tryIt: Open the repo in your AI tool and type /setup. Or edit config.json on GitHub.com -- fill in every field.

nodes.title: Make your first commit
nodes.description: After running /setup, your config.json has changed. Committing saves that change with a description of what you did and why. This is the core rhythm of GitHub: change something, describe the change, save it. Every commit is a point in time you can return to.
nodes.tryIt: If you edited config.json on GitHub.com, you already committed. If you used an AI tool locally, commit now with a message like: 'configure my OS with personal context'

nodes.title: Try your first slash command
nodes.description: Slash commands are quick actions your AI follows. Try /brainstorm or /plan with your 30-day goal. The AI reads your config.json, so it already knows your context. This is the first taste of how the OS makes AI useful.
nodes.tryIt: Type: /plan [your 30-day goal from config.json]. See how the AI uses your context to give you a tailored plan.

nodes.title: Understand agents.md and CLAUDE.md
nodes.description: agents.md is the universal config that tells any AI tool how to behave in your repo. CLAUDE.md adds Claude-specific features on top. Together, they're like onboarding docs for your AI assistant. Edit them to change how AI helps you.
nodes.tryIt: Open agents.md and add one line under 'Behavior' that reflects how you like to work. Example: '- When I ask for help with writing, draft the full piece instead of an outline.'

nodes.title: Track a task with GitHub Issues
nodes.description: GitHub Issues is your built-in task tracker. Each issue is a task, idea, or question with its own discussion thread. Your repo comes with templates for ideas, tasks, and questions so you don't start from a blank page. Better than sticky notes, cheaper than project management software.
nodes.tryIt: Go to your repo's Issues tab. Click 'New issue', pick the 'Task' template, and create an issue for something you need to do this week.

nodes.title: Write your first journal entry
nodes.description: The journal/ folder is your thinking space. Write daily notes, weekly reflections, ideas, lessons learned. There are no rules -- it's for you. The value builds over time: searchable, versioned, always accessible. Your AI tools can also read it for context.
nodes.tryIt: Create a file called journal/week-1.md. Write 3 sentences about what you're working on this week and what you hope to accomplish.

nodes.title: Log a decision
nodes.description: Six months from now you'll ask 'why did I do it this way?' The decision log answers that. Copy the template in docs/decisions/, fill it out for a real decision you've made recently. Context, decision, reasoning, consequences.
nodes.tryIt: Copy docs/decisions/000-template.md to docs/decisions/001-chose-github-os.md. Fill it out: why did you decide to use GitHub OS? What alternatives did you consider?

nodes.title: Document a process you repeat
nodes.description: If you do something more than twice, write it down. SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures) go in docs/sops/. Could be how you onboard a client, how you publish content, how you handle support requests. Written in markdown, versioned automatically.
nodes.tryIt: Use /write to draft an SOP: '/write an SOP for [something I do regularly]'. Save it to docs/sops/.

nodes.title: Set up a Project board
nodes.description: GitHub Projects gives you a kanban board connected to your issues. Drag tasks between To Do, In Progress, and Done. It's free Trello, built into GitHub, and stays in sync with your issues automatically.
nodes.tryIt: Go to your repo's Projects tab, create a new Board project, and add your existing issues to it. Drag one to 'In Progress'.

nodes.title: Get feedback with /review
nodes.description: The /review command gives you structured feedback on anything -- a document, a plan, a piece of writing, code. It returns 3 strengths, 3 improvements, and a suggested next step. Use it before you share or ship anything.
nodes.tryIt: Pick something you've written in your repo (a journal entry, SOP, or decision log) and run: /review [filename]

tracks.title: Build & Extend
tracks.description: Add your own tools and automations

nodes.title: Deploy your roadmap site
nodes.description: Your repo includes a roadmap site in apps/roadmap/ and a GitHub Action that auto-deploys it. Enable GitHub Pages in your repo settings and your progress dashboard goes live at yourname.github.io/github-os. Every push to apps/roadmap/ triggers a fresh deploy.
nodes.tryIt: Go to Settings > Pages. Set source to 'GitHub Actions'. Push any change to apps/roadmap/ and watch it deploy. Visit yourname.github.io/github-os to see it live.

nodes.title: Add your first project to apps/
nodes.description: The apps/ folder is where your projects live. Each one gets its own folder with its own README. Could be a landing page, a tool, a side project, a client site. The repo structure keeps everything organized in one place.
nodes.tryIt: Create apps/my-first-project/README.md. Write one sentence about what the project is. Use /plan to break it into phases.

nodes.title: Create a workflow template
nodes.description: Workflows are documented processes in workflows/. Your repo includes templates for content pipelines and project kickoffs. Create your own for any process you repeat: client onboarding, weekly review, content publishing, sales follow-up.
nodes.tryIt: Use /write to draft a workflow: '/write a workflow template for [your repeatable process]'. Save to workflows/templates/.

nodes.title: Create a custom slash command
nodes.description: Commands are just markdown files in .claude/commands/. Create a new .md file, write instructions for what it should do, and it becomes available as a slash command. No code needed. Ideas: /daily for a morning standup, /reflect for end-of-week review, /pitch for elevator pitch practice.
nodes.tryIt: Create .claude/commands/daily.md with instructions like: 'Summarize my recent commits, check open issues, and suggest what I should focus on today.'

nodes.title: Create a custom skill
nodes.description: Skills are deeper than commands. A skill teaches your AI how to do something specific -- like reviewing content against your brand guidelines, generating meeting agendas, or following your writing style. Each skill is a SKILL.md file in a folder.
nodes.tryIt: Create .claude/skills/my-style/SKILL.md with instructions for how you like to write. Include examples of your voice, words you use, words you avoid.

nodes.title: Set up a GitHub Action
nodes.description: Actions are automations triggered by events in your repo. Push code? Auto-deploy. Create an issue? Send a notification. Your repo already has one (deploy-roadmap.yml). Read it to understand the pattern, then create your own.
nodes.tryIt: Read .github/workflows/deploy-roadmap.yml to understand the structure. Then search the GitHub Actions marketplace for an automation that would save you time.

nodes.title: Use branches for experiments
nodes.description: A branch is a copy of your project where you can try things without affecting the main version. Want to reorganize your docs? Try it on a branch. If it works, merge it in. If not, delete the branch. No harm done.
nodes.tryIt: Create a branch called 'experiment'. Make a change, commit it, then view the branch on GitHub to see how it differs from main.

nodes.title: Collaborate with Pull Requests
nodes.description: Pull Requests are how changes get reviewed before merging into main. Even solo, they create a record of what changed and why. For teams, they're essential. No one changes the main branch without review.
nodes.tryIt: From your experiment branch, open a Pull Request. Write a description of what you changed and why. Merge it when you're satisfied.

nodes.title: Share your skills and commands
nodes.description: Skills and commands are just files. Share them by linking to your repo, or create a separate template repo of your best skills. Others can copy them into their own OS. This is how the ecosystem grows.
nodes.tryIt: Pick your best custom command or skill. Share the GitHub link with someone who has their own OS repo and help them add it.

nodes.title: Add a custom domain
nodes.description: Instead of yourname.github.io, point your own domain at your GitHub Pages site. Free hosting with your own URL. Makes your roadmap or any app you build look professional.
nodes.tryIt: If you own a domain, go to Settings > Pages > Custom domain and follow the DNS setup instructions.

nodes.title: Customize your issue templates
nodes.description: Your repo came with templates for ideas, tasks, and questions. Customize them for your actual workflow, or add new ones. If you onboard clients, make a 'New client' template. If you produce content, make a 'Content brief' template.
nodes.tryIt: Open .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/ and edit one of the existing templates, or create a new one that fits your workflow.

nodes.title: Invite a collaborator
nodes.description: When you're ready to go multiplayer, invite someone to your repo. They can create issues, submit PRs, and use the same skills and commands. This is how a personal OS becomes a team OS.
nodes.tryIt: Go to Settings > Collaborators and invite someone you work with. Create an issue and assign it to them.
